content:
Users wanted live updates — I delivered without breaking a sweat.

7 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

I remember the exact moment I realized traditional HTTP wasn’t going to cut it anymore. I was building a live dashboard for a client who needed real-time metrics — stock prices, user activity, system health. Every second of delay meant lost money. Every refresh button was a user frustration waiting to happen.
I started with polling. Every 500 milliseconds, the client would ask the server, “Anything new?” The server would say “no” 99% of the time. It was like calling your friend every five seconds to ask if they’d arrived yet — inefficient, annoying, and completely unnecessary.
Then I discovered WebSockets. And everything changed.
Here’s how I build real-time apps with WebSockets and Node.js — without breaking a sweat.
Why WebSockets? The Problem with HTTP
Traditional HTTP follows a simple pattern: the client asks, the server answers. The client is always the one initiating. This works fine for static pages, but for real-time applications, it’s a disaster.
Polling is the naive solution. The client asks the server every few seconds, “Anything new?” This creates constant…
